steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks for the function of the Algeria information within the text as a whole. Algeria's drop in solid-fuel use appears first; the text then introduces the energy ladder model as a common explanation for such shifts, and finally uses Heltberg's Ghana study to argue the energy ladder is "reductive." Algeria's role is to set up the phenomenon the text critiques.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'It provides an example of a type of change that the text goes on to suggest is poorly suited for evaluating whether the energy ladder is a viable model.': the text never says Algeria is a bad test case for the energy ladder — it uses Algeria as a prime example of the kind of shift the energy ladder is meant to explain. ✗
  • Choice B — 'It introduces a finding that the text goes on to suggest can be explained in two different ways that are equally compelling.': the text does not present two equally compelling explanations; it argues that the energy ladder explanation is reductive and that the multi-factor view (Ghana) is more accurate. ✗
  • Choice C — 'It describes a trend that the text goes on to suggest has a similar cause as a seemingly unrelated trend observed in Ghana.': the text contrasts Algeria (simple ladder explanation) with Ghana (complex, multi-factor reality) — it does not suggest they share the same cause. ✗
  • Choice D — 'It illustrates the kind of phenomenon that the text goes on to suggest is frequently but inadequately accounted for by the energy ladder.': Algeria's fuel-use shift is exactly the kind of phenomenon the energy ladder is "often" invoked to explain, and the Ghana study shows that explanation is reductive — so the ladder accounts for such shifts frequently but inadequately. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
